feed_description: Demo watchlist for Missouri LTC pharmacy operator intel.

items.title: MO HealthNet nursing-facility rate pressure keeps Medicaid-heavy accounts cost-sensitive
items.summary: Missouri nursing facilities remain reimbursement-sensitive, which tends to increase scrutiny of pharmacy spend and non-formulary use.
items.operator_implication: Operators are likely to push harder on cost discipline, emergency delivery charges, and vendor concessions.
items.pharmacy_implication: Expect more therapeutic-substitution requests, tighter invoice review, and greater waste-reduction pressure.
items.suggested_action: Review Medicaid-heavy accounts for margin exposure and prep formulary/waste-reduction talking points.

items.title: Survey pressure keeps med documentation and consultant follow-up under the microscope
items.summary: Missouri SNFs continue to face a high-visibility survey environment around infection control, psychotropics, med storage, and documentation quality.
items.operator_implication: Survey heat raises urgency around clean corrective action, leadership responsiveness, and fewer avoidable med errors.
items.pharmacy_implication: Facilities will value faster documentation support, med-room audit help, and stronger consultant pharmacist execution.
items.suggested_action: Flag accounts with survey vulnerability and prioritize psychotropic review, med-storage support, and documentation cleanup.

items.title: Respiratory and infectious-disease readiness belongs on the Missouri watchboard
items.summary: Even absent a crisis headline, LTC operators need active readiness for flu, COVID, RSV, norovirus, and other fast-moving facility pathogens.
items.operator_implication: Outbreaks disrupt staffing, admissions, family communication, and survey confidence.
items.pharmacy_implication: Can drive spikes in antiviral, symptom-management, vaccine, and urgent-delivery demand.
items.suggested_action: Keep outbreak-readiness cards visible: emergency stock, immunization outreach, and urgent-delivery contingency status.
items.sample_source_type: MO DHSS / CDC / local public-health advisories

items.title: Administrator and DON churn remain strong predictors of account pain
items.summary: The most difficult accounts are often those with leadership churn, agency dependence, and weak workflow discipline rather than simple census issues.
items.operator_implication: Labor fragility increases execution failures, family complaints, and readmission risk.
items.pharmacy_implication: These buildings generate refill misses, order clarifications, missing-dose noise, and billing disputes.
items.suggested_action: Tag labor-fragile accounts for extra onboarding, tighter cycle-fill controls, and proactive check-ins.

items.title: Ownership transfer, closure, or receivership should auto-elevate to critical
items.summary: Facility transition events create resident-transfer chaos, med-custody risk, controlled-substance accountability questions, and A/R exposure.
items.operator_implication: Leadership attention gets pulled into transfers, regulators, families, and admissions disruption.
items.pharmacy_implication: Pharmacy needs a clean transition playbook for cart reconciliation, destruction logs, transfer fills, and custody documentation.
items.suggested_action: Create a transition checklist card and auto-highlight any served account with ownership/closure distress signals.
items.sample_source_type: local reporting / regulator notices / court filings

items.title: Psychotropic oversight remains a durable compliance signal
items.summary: Antipsychotic use, gradual dose reduction, indication clarity, and behavioral documentation remain recurring areas of scrutiny.
items.operator_implication: These issues influence survey performance, clinical oversight, and family confidence.
items.pharmacy_implication: Strong consultant-pharmacist support here helps pharmacy look like an operator partner rather than a commodity vendor.
items.suggested_action: Surface facilities with elevated psychotropic-review burden or repeated documentation gaps.

items.title: Competitor expansion and service-line claims should be tracked as account-risk signals
items.summary: Executive hires, new routes, packaging promises, and clinical-service claims can foreshadow account pursuit in Missouri.
items.operator_implication: Stronger operators compare vendors on responsiveness, consultant support, and problem-solving, not just price.
items.pharmacy_implication: Competitive motion can precede churn risk or pricing pressure at target and existing accounts.
items.suggested_action: Maintain a lightweight competitor lane covering hires, expansion, route density, and service claims.

items.title: Federal staffing-policy pressure still changes Missouri operator behavior
items.summary: Even when implementation details move, staffing-policy pressure has already raised operator sensitivity to workflow friction and visible staffing weakness.
items.operator_implication: Facilities are planning for tighter scrutiny and need vendors that reduce daily operational drag.
items.pharmacy_implication: Pharmacy can win by simplifying med-pass workflows, exception handling, and delivery reliability for thinly staffed buildings.
items.suggested_action: Position the command center as a workflow-friction reducer for labor-stressed facilities.

willy_handoff.ux_note: Use badges for priority + category, then hide longer implications in an expander or modal.
willy_handoff.safety_note: Label as demo/sample intel until live data sources are wired.
